title: Adult Cancer Screening: Age-Appropriate Early Detection
subtitle: A 52-year-old woman (former 20 pack-year smoker, quit 5 years ago) is at a wellness visit. The nurse teaches which cancer screenings are due — colorectal, breast, cervical, and lung — and that screening finds cancer before symptoms.

patient.name: Cancer Screening Visit
patient.admitDate: Wellness visit. 52F, average risk, former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5y ago), sister with breast cancer. Reviewing age-appropriate cancer screening.

vitals.temp: Start age 45; colonoscopy q10y or FIT yearly

labs.v: Colonoscopy q10y; FIT yearly; stool-DNA q3y.

nursesNote: A 52-year-old woman presents for a wellness visit. She is average risk for most cancers, is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago), and has a sister with breast cancer. The nurse reviews age-appropriate cancer screening: colorectal screening begins at age 45 (colonoscopy every 10 years, or an annual FIT, or stool-DNA every 3 years); mammography is recommended for women roughly 40–74; cervical screening uses a Pap and/or HPV test and usually stops around age 65; and because of her smoking history, annual low-dose CT lung screening is indicated for ages 50–80 with at least a 20 pack-year history. The nurse emphasizes that screening detects cancer before symptoms appear, that a positive screening test requires diagnostic follow-up, and that family history may warrant earlier or more frequent screening.
providerOrders: Review age and risk factors to determine due screenings
Discuss purpose, options, and prep for each indicated screening
Schedule colorectal, breast, cervical, and lung screening as due
Arrange diagnostic follow-up for any abnormal screening result
Document teaching and next-due screening dates

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about cancer screening?

opts.text: Screening aims to detect cancer before symptoms appear
Average-risk colorectal screening begins at age 45
You only need screening once you have symptoms
Mammography is the recommended breast-cancer screening test
A 20 pack-year smoker aged 50–80 may qualify for annual low-dose CT lung screening
Feeling well means no screening is needed
A positive screening test requires diagnostic follow-up (e.g., colonoscopy after a positive FIT)
Family history can warrant earlier or more frequent screening
Cervical screening can usually stop around age 65 after adequate prior screening
All cancer screening continues for life regardless of age or health
Cervical screening options include a Pap, HPV testing, or co-testing
A screening test by itself confirms a cancer diagnosis
Review the patient's age, history, and risk factors
Identify which screenings are due (colorectal, breast, cervical, lung)
Explain the purpose and options for each screening
Help schedule the due screenings and address barriers
Reinforce follow-up for any positive result
Colorectal screening (colonoscopy or FIT)
Mammography for breast-cancer screening
Cervical screening with a Pap and/or HPV test
Annual low-dose CT lung screening
Routine whole-body MRI to screen for any cancer
Skipping all screening because she feels healthy
A follow-up colonoscopy after a positive FIT
Waiting for symptoms before any screening

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm which screenings are due based on age and risk
Teach the purpose, options, and prep for each
Order or schedule the indicated screenings with the provider
Set up diagnostic follow-up for any abnormal result
Document teaching and set reminders for next-due dates
"Screening can find cancer early, before I have symptoms."
"I should start colon screening at 45 since I'm average risk."
"A positive stool test means I'll need a colonoscopy to look closer."
"I feel fine, so I'll skip all my screenings."
"Since I'm 52, I can stop cervical screening now."
"Because I smoked, a yearly low-dose CT scan is recommended for me."
"A screening test by itself proves I have cancer."
"My sister's breast cancer might mean I need earlier or more screening."

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Health Promotion and Maintenance, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
